topic: mechanical keyboard deep dive — Cherry MX vs Gateron

turns.content: settle a debate for me. cherry mx browns or gateron browns

Oh, the eternal tactile switch debate. Short answer: Gateron Browns are smoother out of the box, Cherry MX Browns have a slightly more defined tactile bump. What's driving the question — are you building a new board or thinking about swapping switches?

thinking about swapping. i've been on cherry mx browns for like 4 years and they're fine but i keep reading that gaterons are just... better? smoother, less scratchy, cheaper. feels like cherry is coasting on brand recognition at this point

You're not wrong about the coasting. Cherry has improved their recent batches — the Hyperglide and new-tooling switches are smoother than the older ones — but stock-for-stock, Gateron Browns do feel smoother and have less spring ping. The tactile bump is slightly more pronounced on Cherrys though. Gateron Browns are sometimes called "barely tactile" by enthusiasts.

see that's my concern. i actually want to feel the bump. the whole point of tactile is that i know the key actuated without bottoming out. if i wanted linear i'd just get reds. what about gateron pro browns, any different?

Gateron Pro Browns are a step up — factory pre-lubed and the bump is slightly more defined than standard Gaterons. Still not as crisp as Cherry's bump, but noticeably smoother. If you want a genuinely strong tactile feel though, you might want to look beyond both brands entirely. Glorious Pandas or Durock T1 switches have much more pronounced tactility.

hmm i don't think i want to go full enthusiast territory. i just want something i can throw in a board and type on for 10 hours a day without thinking about it. i code, i don't write poetry. wait actually — have you tried akko cs lavender purples? someone at work was recommending those

Akko CS Lavender Purples are a great budget tactile. Sharper bump than either Cherry or Gateron browns, and Akko's factory lube is decent. Total travel is 3mm instead of 4mm, which some people love for fast typing and others find jarring. For marathon coding sessions, the shorter travel can reduce finger fatigue. At around 25 cents per switch, they're hard to beat on value.

shorter travel might actually be nice. i bottom out on every keystroke anyway so the last mm is basically wasted on me. what about the sound profile though? i'm in an open office sometimes and i don't want to be that guy with the machine gun keyboard

The Akkos are a bit louder on bottom-out than Cherry Browns due to the shorter travel — you hit the housing sooner and harder. But that's more about your board and keycaps than the switch itself. If you put them in a board with good dampening and maybe add some PE foam or tape mod, they'll be quieter than stock Cherrys. What board are you using?

keychron q1 with hotswap. so at least i don't need to desolder anything. i did desolder a whole board once at like 2am and it was weirdly meditative. like the hardware equivalent of refactoring legacy code

Ha — 2am desoldering as meditation tracks. The Q1 is perfect for switch experiments since you can just pull and swap. It also has decent stock dampening already. One suggestion: grab a pack of 45 Akkos first and swap just your home row and modifiers. Type on them for a week before committing to a full set. That way you can evaluate the shorter travel with minimal investment.

that's smart actually. worst case i'm out like 15 euros for a pack. and if the travel feels weird on home row i'll know immediately. okay one more question — o-rings on tactile switches, yes or no? i've seen people do it to dampen the bottom-out but it sounds like it would ruin the feel

Generally no on o-rings with tactiles. They shorten the already-short travel, make the bottom-out feel mushy, and can reduce the perceived tactile bump because you're decelerating into rubber instead of getting a clean stop. If noise is the concern, you're better off with switch films and a silicone dampener in the case. The Q1 also responds well to the tape mod on the PCB back — cheap and effective.

okay good that confirms my instinct. o-rings felt like a hack and not the good kind. alright i'm ordering the akko pack tonight. if this goes well i might finally have an excuse to build a second board for home. which my wallet will not appreciate but my fingers will

The second board slippery slope is real — once you start optimizing switches for different use cases, suddenly you need a "work board" and a "home board" and maybe a travel board. But honestly, if you're typing 10+ hours a day, having a setup that feels right is a legitimate investment. Enjoy the Akkos — report back once you've lived with them for a week.
